title: Central Venous Access Device (CVAD) Care and Maintenance
subtitle: The nurse maintains a central line safely — using sterile technique for dressing changes, scrubbing the hub before access, flushing with saline, clamping to prevent air embolism, and recognizing a line infection.

patient.name: Central Line Care
patient.admitDate: The nurse cares for a central venous access device, including dressing changes and flushing.

nursesNote: The nurse maintains a central venous access device (CVAD) such as a PICC, tunneled catheter, or implanted port, which ends in a large central vein for long-term therapy. Strict sterile technique is used for dressing changes, with a mask on both the nurse and the patient; the skin is cleansed with chlorhexidine using friction and allowed to dry before the new dressing is applied. A transparent dressing is changed about every 7 days (gauze every 2 days) and immediately if it is loose, soiled, or damp. Before every access the nurse scrubs the hub with alcohol or chlorhexidine for about 15 seconds and lets it dry, then flushes with normal saline using positive pressure to keep the line patent; resistance is never forced, since that can dislodge a clot. The line is kept clamped or capped when not in use and tubing is primed to prevent an air embolism. PICC tip placement is confirmed by X-ray before first use. The nurse assesses the site for redness, swelling, drainage, or tenderness and reports fever, which can signal a central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI); the catheter is removed as soon as it is no longer needed.
providerOrders: Use sterile technique with masks for central line dressing changes
Cleanse with chlorhexidine and allow it to dry
Scrub the hub before each access and flush with normal saline
Keep the line clamped or capped when not in use
Assess the site and report signs of a CLABSI; remove the line when no longer needed

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about central line care?

opts.text: A central line ends in a large central vein for long-term access
Sterile technique is used for central line dressing changes
The hub is scrubbed before each access
The line is flushed with normal saline to maintain patency
Clean (non-sterile) gloves are enough for a dressing change
The dressing is changed only after it falls off completely
Chlorhexidine must dry before the dressing is applied
Scrubbing the hub keeps bacteria out of the bloodstream
A line left open to air risks an air embolism
Forcing fluid against resistance safely clears a clot
Redness, drainage, or fever can signal a CLABSI
Removing the line when no longer needed lowers infection risk

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order a central line dressing change

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene and apply masks to nurse and patient
Open the sterile kit and don sterile gloves
Remove the old dressing and assess the site
Cleanse with chlorhexidine and allow it to dry
Apply the new sterile dressing and label it
Scrubbing the hub before each access
Using clean technique for the dressing change
Letting chlorhexidine dry before applying the dressing
Leaving the line uncapped and open to air
Flushing with normal saline to keep the line patent
Forcing the flush against strong resistance
Changing a damp or loose dressing right away
Applying a dressing without dating or labeling it

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The site is red with purulent drainage and the patient is febrile
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the site and obtain vital signs
Notify the provider of a suspected CLABSI
Obtain blood cultures as ordered
Prepare to remove the catheter per orders
Document the findings
Scrubbing the hub and letting it dry before access
Using clean gloves for a central line dressing change
Changing the transparent dressing about every 7 days
Leaving a wet, peeling dressing in place
Flushing with saline using positive pressure
Forcing fluid past resistance to clear a clot
Clamping the line when it is not in use
Leaving the catheter in after it is no longer needed

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
